coredata.dc:title: Electrochemiluminescence detection of catechol and tryptophol using nitrogen, sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots based on a paper-based sensor
coredata.dc:creator: Zhi, Shaoze
Zhu, Zikang
Li, Yipeng
Yang, Zhengchun
Liu, Jun
Shi, Junjun
Pan, Peng
Qi, Yangyang
He, Jie
coredata.dc:description: In this study, Nitrogen and Sulfur co-doped Graphene Quantum Dots (N, S-GQDs) were synthesized via a pyrolysis approach, utilizing citric acid as the carbon source and L-cysteine as the nitrogen and sulfur sources. These N, S-GQDs were used to develop a novel paper-based electrochemiluminescence (ECL) sensor for the detection of catechol (CC) and tryptophol, two potentially hazardous substances in the environment. Screen printing technology was utilized to construct the ECL sensor on filter paper, incorporating a hydrophobic layer, working and counter electrodes, and a reference electrode. The presence of CC and tryptophol was found to result in a decrease in the ECL intensity of the quantum dots (QDs), this quenching phenomenon is attributed to the oxidation of these substances within the electrochemical system and the subsequent electron transfer from the excited state of the QDs to the oxidation products. The sensor demonstrated a linear detection range for CC and tryptophol from 0.01 to 1000 μM, with detection limits of 0.0082 μM and 0.0066 μM, respectively, and exhibited excellent selectivity, stability, and repeatability. This cost-effective and efficient monitoring technique holds significant promise for environmental and health safety by facilitating the detection of CC and tryptophol, thereby mitigating their potential adverse impacts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
N, S-GQDs
Catechol detection
Tryptophol detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Stability and selectivity of N, S-GQDs in electrochemiluminescence
Detection of catechol and tryptophol by using N, S-GQDs
Principle of using N, S-GQDs to detect catechol and tryptophol
Repeatability, stability and feasibility of ECL sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM and XPS analyses of N, S-GQDs. Fig. 1. (a) and (b) display the TEM and HRTEM images of N, S-GQDs. Fig. 1. (c) - (f) present the XPS spectra of N, S-GQDs.
(a) UV–vis spectra of N, S-GQDs, with inset showing N, S-GQDs under 365 nm UV light, (b) fluorescence spectra of N, S-GQDs.
(a) ECL emission of N, S-GQDs under continuous 12 potential scans, (b) selectivity of the prepared sensor towards interferents.
Comparison of with and without addition of CC and tryptophol. (a) and (b) CV curves, (c) and (d) ECL responses, (e) and (f) the CV curve of the analyte at a concentration of 10-3 M.
(a) and (c): The ECL responses of CC and tryptophol at concentrations ranging from 0.01 to 1000 μM. (b) and (d): The logarithmic calibration curves for the detection of CC and tryptophol.
Comparison of the proposed method with other published techniques for CC detection.
Comparison of the proposed method with other published techniques for tryptophol detection.

head.ce:title: Electrochemiluminescence detection of catechol and tryptophol using nitrogen, sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots based on a paper-based sensor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Integrated Circuit Science and Engineering, Advanced Materials and Printed Electronics Center, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Film Electronic & Communication Devices, Tianjin University of Technology, Tianjin 300384, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Integrated Circuit Science and Engineering, Advanced Materials and Printed Electronics Center
Tianjin Key Laboratory of Film Electronic & Communication Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Integrated Circuit Science and Engineering, Advanced Materials and Printed Electronics Center, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Film Electronic & Communication Devices, Tianjin University of Technology, Tianjin 300384, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electrical Engineering and Automation, Tianjin University of Technology, Tianjin 300384, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electrical Engineering and Automation

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Electrical Engineering and Automation, Tianjin University of Technology, Tianjin 300384, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Sinopec Research Institute of Petroleum Co., Ltd., Beijing 100083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sinopec Research Institute of Petroleum Co., Ltd.

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Sinopec Research Institute of Petroleum Co., Ltd., Beijing 100083, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots via pyrolysis method using citric acid as a carbon source and L-cysteine as a sulfur and nitrogen source, for the detection of catechol and tryptophol on paper-based electrodes.

In this study, Nitrogen and Sulfur co-doped Graphene Quantum Dots (N, S-GQDs) were synthesized via a pyrolysis approach, utilizing citric acid as the carbon source and L-cysteine as the nitrogen and sulfur sources. These N, S-GQDs were used to develop a novel paper-based electrochemiluminescence (ECL) sensor for the detection of catechol (CC) and tryptophol, two potentially hazardous substances in the environment. Screen printing technology was utilized to construct the ECL sensor on filter paper, incorporating a hydrophobic layer, working and counter electrodes, and a reference electrode. The presence of CC and tryptophol was found to result in a decrease in the ECL intensity of the quantum dots (QDs), this quenching phenomenon is attributed to the oxidation of these substances within the electrochemical system and the subsequent electron transfer from the excited state of the QDs to the oxidation products. The sensor demonstrated a linear detection range for CC and tryptophol from 0.01 to 1000 μM, with detection limits of 0.0082 μM and 0.0066 μM, respectively, and exhibited excellent selectivity, stability, and repeatability. This cost-effective and efficient monitoring technique holds significant promise for environmental and health safety by facilitating the detection of CC and tryptophol, thereby mitigating their potential adverse impacts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As people's lifestyles change [1], environmental protection and physical health have become the pursued way of life [2]. However, this pursuit is challenged by the presence of CC and Candida albicans [3,4]. CC, a compound formed by the substitution of two adjacent hydrogen atoms with hydroxyl groups in the benzene ring, represents a pivotal chemical entity with the capability to synthesize essential chemical materials such as pesticides, dyes, coating stabilizers, rubber curing agents, and electroplating additives. it has important applications in many fields [5]. However, CC is inevitably released into the environment and becomes an environmental pollutant in industrial wastewater. CC is difficult to degrade and remove from wastewater, which pollutes the ecological environment and poses a risk to human health [6,7]. Even ultra-low concentrations of CC can cause cancer, and it was classified as a Class 2B carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer of the World Health Organization.

With the wide application of glucocorticoids, broad-spectrum antibiotics, and immunosuppressive agents in clinical practice, the development of new technologies such as organ and bone marrow transplantation and interventional diagnosis and treatment, and the increasing number of patients with immunodeficiency diseases, the number of people infected with invasive Candida albicans is increasing year by year [8]. The molecular structure of tryptophol comprises an indole ring and an alcohol group attached to the carbon atom of the indole ring. Due to the relatively backward understanding and research of the biology of Candida albicans, people's awareness of prevention is generally weak, and Candida albicans is having an increasingly significant impact on people's health. Currently, there is a lack of effective detection methods for Candida albicans, making it difficult to control its spread. Direct detection of Candida albicans is costly and cumbersome [9,10]. However, it has been found that Candida albicans produces quorum sensing molecules in its physiological process, including alcohols such as tryptophol [11,12], farnesol [13], phenylethanol [14] and tyrosol [15,16], providing a new approach for detecting Candida albicans [17]. By detecting these substances, we can achieve the purpose of detecting Candida albicans. By reading the literature [18], we learned that tryptophol is a potential candidate for detecting Candida albicans, so we selected it as the research object.

Given the significant environmental and health hazards posed by certain substances, the development of precise and sensitive methodologies for their detection is imperative. Currently available techniques, such as electrochemical methods, chromatography, and fluorescence analysis, are widely employed [19–22]. Nonetheless, these methodologies are hampered by limitations including prohibitive costs, constrained linear ranges, operational complexity, and suboptimal sensitivity. Among these, electrochemical approaches are distinguished by their simplicity and cost-efficiency, offering substantial benefits over alternative detection strategies. ECL technology, a sophisticated extension of electrochemical methods, is characterized by its reduced background noise, which facilitates enhanced sensitivity. Furthermore, ECL technology is noted for its accelerated reaction kinetics in comparison to conventional electrochemical methodologies, positioning it as a superior technique for the precise and expedited detection of analytes such as CC and tryptophol [23]. Recent advancements have highlighted the application of ECL in the detection of CC. A notable example is the method developed by Zhou et al., which leverages the synergistic effects between carbon dots and a metal–organic framework (HKUST-1), demonstrating efficacy within a detection range of 2.5 × 10-5 to 5 × 10-9 M and achieving a detection limit of 3.8 × 10-9 M [24]. Other researchers, such as Liu and Chen, have also employed ECL methods for the detection of CC [25,26]. These scholarly advancements have markedly advanced the detection of CC and have contributed to pioneering research methodologies for the application of ECL technology in tryptophol analysis. However, the complex requirements for sample preparation and handling, along with associated logistical challenges, constitute a critical limitation that directly impacts the rapid response of detection processes. Consequently, it is essential to undertake methodological refinements to augment the versatility and applicability of this technology in diverse analytical scenarios.

Since the seminal work by Bard et al. in 2002, which introduced ECL from silicon nanocrystals [27], ECL phenomena have been observed in a variety of materials, including Ru(bpy)3 2+, nanocarbon, and GQDs, under specific conditions [24,28,29]. For instance, Lu et al. have recently engineered a composite material utilizing Ru(bpy)3 2+/MOF (Ru-MOF) for the creation of an ECL sensor aimed at detecting prostate-specific antigen (PSA). Experimental results confirm that the inclusion of PSA leads to a decrease in the system's luminescence intensity. The sensor exhibits a detection span spanning from 5 μg ml−1 to 5 pg ml−1, attaining a minimal detection limit of 1.783 pg mL−1 [30]. This achievement, which melds ECL technology with medical diagnostics, undoubtedly garners acclaim for its innovative approach. Although Ru(bpy)3 2+ and nanocarbon materials have been extensively explored for their ECL applications, they encounter challenges such as limited storage stability, suboptimal luminescence performance, and complexity in handling. In contrast, GQDs exhibit superior ECL characteristics, securing a distinctive position in this domain [31]. Nonetheless, GQDs still face limitations in photoluminescence efficiency, selectivity, and modulation techniques. Notably, studies have revealed that doping GQDs with specific elements can effectively enhance their optical properties, significantly improving both efficiency and selectivity [32,33]. This advancement not only highlights the potential of GQDs in addressing existing challenges but also opens new avenues for further enhancing the performance of ECL systems. As exemplified by, Zhu et al. developed a novel approach for synthesizing nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) through a thermal decomposition technique, leveraging citric acid and urea. The synthesized N-GQDs were subsequently integrated into a paper-based ECL sensor, demonstrating their application in the detection of copper ions. To further understand the material's properties, the team conducted first-principles calculations, shedding light on the electronic and optical behaviors of the N-GQDs. Notably, the N-GQDs displayed green fluorescence when exposed to long-wave ultraviolet light. Through experimental analysis, Zhu et al. established a linear relationship between the ECL signal's intensity and the concentration of copper ions, with the sensor's detection capability ranging from 0.01 μM to 1000 μM. This work not only provides insights into the material properties of N-GQDs but also showcases their potential utility in environmental monitoring and heavy metal detection [34]. Similar experiments have been investigated by other researchers as well. Kalhori et al. demonstrated a synthesis method for N, S-GQDs by dissolving 0.21 g of citric acid (1 mM) and 0.23 g of thiourea (3 mM) in 5 mL of deionized water, followed by extensive stirring to ensure a homogeneous solution. This solution was then heated in a high-pressure autoclave at 160 °C for four hours. The nanostructures obtained were isolated using ethanol as a dispersing medium and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min, resulting in a solid that was easily dispersible in water to form an aqueous solution of N, S-GQDs. These quantum dots were utilized to modify a glassy carbon electrode for the selective detection of iron cations, enhancing electron transfer reactions with ferrous ions due to the quantum dots' free electrons and increased surface area. This modification led to improved sensitivity and a significantly lowered detection limit. The study observed a linear response in the amperometric detection of iron ions across a range of 1 to 100 nM, achieving a notably low detection limit of 0.23 nM [35].

Leveraging the exceptional capabilities of N, S-GQDs, our study aims to use these nanomaterials for the accurate detection of CC and tryptophol. We have achieved the successful development of a detection method distinguished by heightened sensitivity and selectivity. By detecting the presence of CC in water environments, we can promptly monitor and assess the extent of water pollution, providing crucial evidence for safeguarding human health. Furthermore, by measuring the content of tryptophol, we can swiftly discern the existence of Candida albicans within the specimens.The innovation of this study lies in the successful application of N, S-GQDs in the fields of environmental monitoring and bio-diagnosis, achieving fast, sensitive, and reliable detection. These findings provide substantial support for further research and development of efficient methods for water pollution monitoring and Candida albicans diagnosis, with the potential to positively impact environmental protection and public health.

Within the ECL dynamics of N, S-GQDs, the phenomena of both quenching and co-reacting mechanisms play pivotal roles [36,37]. Acknowledging the inherent constraints associated with the quenching mechanism [38], the present investigation adopts the co-reacting mechanism as the strategic approach for the detection of CC and tryptophol. This choice is predicated on leveraging the comparative advantages offered by the co-reacting mechanism, aiming to enhance the specificity and sensitivity of the detection process.

The phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution was purchased from Beijing Solabo Science & Technology Co., Ltd., and potassium persulfate was obtained from Tianjin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Citric acid and L-cysteine were sourced from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Potassium chloride, copper chloride, sodium chloride, magnesium chloride, iron chloride, glucose, dopamine, ascorbic acid, and isoniazid were procured from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. Ethyl cellulose was acquired from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute, while catechol, tryptophol, hydroquinone, phenylethanol, farnesol, tyrosol, turpentine, oil pigments, cellulose paper, carbon ink, and silver ink were supplied by Tianjin KeWei Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.Solvent dilution for the preparation of a 0.01 M PBS buffer solution was carried out using deionized water, with 0.1 M KCl serving as the electrolyte and 0.1 M K2S2O8 acting as a co-reactant. All experimental reagents met analytical grade standards.

N, S-GQDs were produced using a pyrolysis approach. Specifically, a mixture containing 1 g of citric acid and 0.3 g of L-cysteine was heated at 240 °C for 5 min in a porcelain crucible on a heating plate.The resultant yellow–brown liquid was dissolved by slowly adding 10 mL of deionized water to the crucible, forming an orange solution identified as N, S-GQDs. The N, S-GQDs solution underwent dialysis using a Spectra/Por dialysis membrane (MWCO: 2000), yielding a clear solution of N, S-GQDs, which was stored at 2–6 °C.

To enhance experimental efficiency, this study adopted paper-based electrodes as an alternative to traditional electrochemical reactors. The fabrication process involved gradually adding 5 g of ethyl cellulose into a solution containing 100 ml of turpentine oil, followed by magnetic stirring at 60 °C to obtain a colorless, viscous, hydrophobic paste. Subsequently, a yellow oil-based dye was added to this paste. Utilizing screen printing technology, yellow hydrophobic patterns were printed on filter paper to precisely position the electrodes. Next, the three-electrode system was deposited on the paper via screen printing(working and counter electrodes:carbon,reference electrode:Ag/AgCl). By cutting and folding the paper-based chip, a compact reaction chamber featuring three electrodes and a hydrophilic channel was formed, providing a convenient platform for electrochemiluminescence detection. For a detailed description of the fabrication process for paper-based electrodes, please refer to the supplementary materials and references cited therein [39].

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) employing the FEI Talos F200X instrument was utilized for imaging to determine the average size and size distribution of N, S-GQDs. X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were obtained using the SmartLab 9KW X-ray Diffractometer to validate the lattice structure of N, S-GQDs. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured with a METASH UV-5100B spectrophotometer and a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer, respectively. To confirm the incorporation of N and S into the GQD framework, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using the Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi instrument, and XPS spectra were acquired. A more refined cyclic voltammetry (CV) analysis of the 10-3 M analyte was conducted using the CHI660E electrochemical workstation from Shanghai Chenhua. The paper-based electrode used in this study was prepared using a screen printing technique as previously reported in our laboratory and literature.

To prepare the experimental setup, a 50 μL aliquot of the N, S-GQDs solution was first diluted tenfold with 450 μL of phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) buffer. Then, 40 μL of this diluted solution was applied onto the surface of a paper-based working electrode (PWE), thereby forming the N, S-GQDs/PWE assembly. This assembly was subsequently interfaced with the MPI-EII workstation to facilitate the ECL measurement of the N, S-GQDs.Following this, 40 μL of different concentrations (0.1–10000 μM) of CC solution was separately added to 360 μL of the previously diluted N, S-GQDs solution. This resulted in a mixed solution of CC and N, S-GQDs with concentrations ranging from 0.01 to 1000 μM. Subsequently, a 40 μL aliquot of the resulting mixture was deposited onto the surface of the prepared PWE, forming the CC/N, S-GQDs/PWE assembly. Following this, the setup was firmly secured and linked to the ECL analysis apparatus for the assessment of its luminosity intensity.The procedure for detecting tryptophol mirrored that of CC detection, and each experiment was conducted thrice for accuracy. ECL measurements were performed in a 0.01 M PBS (pH 7.0) containing 0.1 M KCl and 0.1 M K2S2O8 as the electrolyte and co-reactant, respectively. The potential was scanned from 0 to 1.7 V at a scan rate of 150 mV s−1, and the photomultiplier tube voltage was maintained at 900 V.

The TEM images depicted in Fig. 1 . (a) and (b) illustrate the synthesized N, S-GQDs with a consistent size. Elevated temperatures during synthesis result in the removal of hydroxyl groups from citric acid, promoting molecular fusion. L-cysteine plays a crucial role in this process, contributing to the formation of N, S-GQDs characterized by high crystallinity and doping with N and S elements. The size distribution analysis presented in Fig. 1. (a) indicates an average particle size of approximately 2.23 nm for N, S-GQDs, with a uniform and relatively compact size distribution ranging from 1.5 to 3 nm, as shown in the inset of Fig. 1.(a). The HRTEM analysis reveals well-defined stripes with a lattice spacing of 0.26 nm, slightly larger than the graphene plane, owing to the incorporation of larger S atoms (Fig. 1. (b)). [40]. Within the XRD pattern of N,S-GQDs (Fig. S3.), a distinctive diffraction peak is discernible at 23° (2θ)., indicative of a layered structure along the 002 direction with an interlayer spacing of approximately 0.26 nm. This indicates that the N,S-GQDs synthesized exhibit a structure resembling graphene with an amorphous carbon component, consistent with findings reported in the literature [41].

To elucidate the elemental composition and functional group structures of N, S-GQDs, XPS was employed for thorough analysis. As depicted in Fig. S4, the XPS full spectrum of N, S-GQDs reveals five distinctive peaks corresponding to O1s, N1s, C1s, S2s, and S2p. This outcome validates the successful incorporation of both N and S into the graphene structure, with elemental contents of C, O, N, and S measuring 74.46 %, 18.69 %, 4.30 %, and 2.55 %, respectively. The relatively low doping content of sulfur in the GQDs can be attributed to the small electronegativity difference between sulfur and carbon, coupled with a significant difference in their atomic sizes. Consequently, the incorporation of sulfur into GQDs is hindered [42,43]. In the high-resolution C1s spectrum depicted in Fig. 1. (c), a dominant peak at 283.9 eV corresponds to the sp2 C = C structure, providing confirmation of the presence of a graphite structure in N, S-GQDs. The peaks observed at 284.9 eV and 287.3 eV can be attributed to sp3 carbon in the C-S, C-N, and C-O configurations, respectively. arise due to the doping of N and S elements and their interaction with –OH groups [44]. The fitted peaks observed in the O1s spectrum at 532.1 and 530.9 eV are indicative of C = O and C-O bonds, respectively (Fig. 1. (e)). In Fig. 1. (d), the peak at 400.8 eV corresponds to graphitic nitrogen, while the peak at 399.1 eV is assigned to pyrrolic nitrogen [45]. The detection of graphitic N and pyrrolic N signifies the successful introduction of nitrogen into the graphite framework. In Fig. 1. (f), the presence of peaks at 163.6 eV and 162.4 eV provides evidence for the successful incorporation of S elements into the graphene structure [46].

Fig. 2 illustrates the UV–vis absorption spectra and optical images of the N, S-GQDs solution when exposed to 365 nm UV light. As portrayed in Fig. 2. (a), the peak at 240 nm is linked to the n-π* transition of the C = O band and the π*-π* transition of the C = C band., serving as an indication of N, S-GQDs formation. Due to the capture of energy from surface-state excitations, the bandgap transition centered at 340 nm exhibits a pronounced fluorescence [32]. Consequently, as depicted in Fig. 2(a), two absorption peaks are evident around 240 and 340 nm. The 240 nm peak is attributed to the π → π* transition of the aromatic sp2 structural domain, and accordingly, no fluorescent signal is observed at 240 nm [47]. Under 340 nm excitation, a distinct light-induced emission with a peak at 435 nm was observed (Fig. 2.(b)). The fluorescence emission of the N, S-GQDs in solution was further characterized in Fig. 2. (b), depicting emissions under varying excitation wavelengths from 310 to 390 nm at 10 nm intervals. Significantly, the emission peaks of the N, S-GQDs solution manifest a discernible shift from 428 nm to 453 nm as the excitation wavelength is varied from 310 nm to 390 nm [48]. The correlation between emission wavelength and intensity with excitation wavelength is a prevalent occurrence in GQDs, possibly arising from their non-uniform surface states or sizes [49].

The variables in the ECL experiments encompass the scan rate, concentration of K2S2O8, and pH value of the PBS solution. Optimization of these factors is essential to establish the most favorable solution environment for analyte detection.The impact of pH values on the ECL intensity of N, S-GQDs is illustrated in Fig. S5. (a) and S5. (b). It is observed that the ECL intensity follows a pattern of increase and then decrease with changes in pH, peaking at pH 7, which indicates the optimal pH condition for the reaction. Fig. S5. (c) and S5. (d) explore how different scan rates affect ECL intensity. The intensity initially increases before declining as the scan rate changes from 0.1 to 0.2 Vs−1, identifying 0.15 Vs−1 as the ideal scan rate. Additionally, Fig. S5. (e) and S5. (f) demonstrate the influence of K2S2O8 concentration on ECL intensity, with 0.10 M being the optimal concentration for achieving peak ECL intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and selectivity of N, S-GQDs in electrochemiluminescence
ce:section.ce:para: N, S-GQDs/PWE were synthesized following the procedures outlined in section 2.4 and affixed to the ECL analysis device for subsequent measurements. The ECL signal of N, S-GQDs was recorded using CV. As shown in Fig. 3. (a), the ECL stability of N, S-GQDs was observed over 12 successive cycles of potential scanning, demonstrating an RSD of 2.3 %. The comprehensive ECL process involving N, S-GQDs and K2S2O8 co-reactants includes the reduction of N, S-GQDs to N, S-GQDs•− and the reduction of the K2S2O8 co-reactant to SO4•−. Subsequently, N, S-GQDs•− reacts with SO4•−, leading to the formation of the free radical N, S-GQDs*. ECL emission occurs as N, S-GQDs* decays back to N, S-GQDs. It is noteworthy that CC is commonly found alongside various pollutants in water environments, including copper ions, iron ions, and hydroquinone.Additionally, the quorum-sensing molecules of Candida albicans also include phenylethanol, tyrosol, and farnesol. To confirm the specificity of the N, S-GQDs-modified paper sensor for CC and tryptophol, we selected different interferents (FeCl3, NaCl, KCl, CuCl2, MgCl2, dopamine (DA), glucose (Glu), isoniazid (INH), H2O2, ascorbic acid (ASC), citric acid (CA), hydroquinone(HQ), phenylethanol, tyrosol, farnesol) at 100 μM and examined their ECL intensities. Fig. 3. (b) demonstrates that the introduction of additional substances has negligible impact on the electrogenerated ECL intensity of the system. In contrast, the addition of 100 μM CC or tryptophol leads to a significant reduction in ECL intensity. These findings confirm the initial selectivity of the N, S-GQDs-modified paper electrode-based ECL sensor for detecting CC and tryptophol. The reason for the selectivity in the detection system lies in the ease of oxidation of CC and tryptophol compared to most other interfering substances. Inorganic substances like metal ions, exhibiting relatively stable chemical properties within this reaction system, do not significantly impact experimental results. Concerning organic compounds, such as DA with a structure similar to tryptophol, electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding lead to the formation of complexes with N, S-GQDs [50,51]. The formation of these complexes hinders the oxidation of DA, making it challenging for DA to generate quinone-like substances similar to benzoquinone. Consequently, this does not exert a discernible influence on the experimental results. For specific experimental outcomes, please refer to the fourth section of the supplementary materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of catechol and tryptophol by using N, S-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 depict the linear detection of N, S-GQDs towards CC and tryptophol, the ECL intensity for each concentration of CC and tryptophol was measured in triplicate for robust experimental validation. As depicted in Fig. 5. (a) and Fig. 5. (c), the ECL intensity exhibits a decrease with increasing concentrations of both CC and tryptophol within the 0.01–1000 μM range. A robust linear correlation between the ECL intensity and the logarithm of their concentrations (X = lg c) is evident in Fig. 5. (b) and Fig. 5. (d), spanning the concentration range of 0.01–1000 μM. The detection limits for CC and tryptophol are 0.0082 μM and 0.0066 μM (S/N = 3), respectively. This concentration-dependent response provides a reliable analytical framework for quantifying CC and tryptophol concentrations over a broad range.

Tables 1 and 2
showcase a comparative analysis of the novel N, S-GQDs modified electrode-based ECL sensor with previously reported methods for detecting CC and tryptophol. The results highlight the proposed sensor's superior performance in detecting CC and tryptophol compared to most existing sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of using N, S-GQDs to detect catechol and tryptophol
ce:section.ce:para: Through experiments, The introduction of CC to N, S-GQDs was found to notably diminish the ECL intensity of CC, as evident from the observations. This is because phenolic compounds and their derivatives can reduce the ECL intensity through mechanisms involving electron transfer [57]. As the scan potential increases from 0 to 1.7 V, CC in the solution undergoes electrochemical oxidation to form quinones. However, when the potential decreases from 1.7 V to 0 V, the system generates SO4•− and N, S-GQDs•−; these species further react to produce excited state N,S-GQD*. The electrons from excited state N,S-GQD* move towards the quinones, resulting in a significant ECL quenching phenomenon [25]. According to literature [18], tryptophol features a hydroxyl group (–OH), and the amino group serves to amplify its electrochemical activity. As depicted in Fig. 4. (f), the prominent oxidation peak in the graph unequivocally verifies the oxidation occurrence of tryptophol within the reaction system, so it is speculated that its reaction mechanism is similar to that of CC. Tryptophol is electrochemically oxidized to aldehyde or other oxidation products in the solution, and the electrons of the excited state QDs move towards the oxidation products, resulting in a decrease in the ECL intensity. The schematic representation of its reaction mechanism is presented in Fig. S7.

To evaluate the sensor's efficacy in practical scenarios, this study conducted detection and recovery experiments with varying concentrations of CC and tryptophol in tap water, comparing the results to those obtained via conventional UV–Vis spectroscopy and the N, S-GQDs modified paper-based ECL sensor. Specifically, aliquots of 10 μL, 50 μL, and 100 μL from a 0.01 M CC solution were added to 9.99 mL, 9.95 mL, and 9.90 mL of tap water, respectively, to prepare solutions with concentrations of 10 μM, 50 μM, and 100 μM. Similarly, for tryptophol, 10 μL, 20 μL, and 30 μL of a 0.01 M solution were introduced into 9.99 mL, 9.98 mL, and 9.97 mL of tap water, respectively, resulting in final concentrations of 10 μM, 20 μM, and 30 μM. Following the methodology described in section 2.4, 40 μL of each prepared solution was applied to the ECL sensor for analysis.The experiments were performed in triplicate for each concentration to ensure reliability, with the ECL intensity meticulously recorded for each sample. Recovery rates and standard deviations were calculated, as detailed in Tables S1 and S2, demonstrating that the sensor's detection recovery rates fell within an acceptable range, indicating high reliability and precision of the sensor. Comparative analysis with UV–Vis spectroscopy revealed no significant discrepancies between the two methods, underscoring the novel paper-based ECL sensor's competence for accurate and efficient detection in real-world applications. This evidence strongly supports the sensor's potential for broader application in environmental monitoring and analytical chemistry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Repeatability, stability and feasibility of ECL sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The assessment of the performance of the ECL sensor is of paramount importance, with stability and reproducibility emerging as pivotal factors in this evaluation. Therefore, we opted to conduct multiple ECL luminescence intensity experiments using N, S-GQDs prepared 60 days prior under identical experimental conditions. Subsequently, these N, S-GQDs were employed for detecting concentrations of CC and tryptophol at 10-3 M and 10-8 M, respectively. As depicted in Fig. S8. (a), the luminescence intensity of N, S-GQDs exhibited slight deviation compared to the previous experimental results. Fig. S8. (b) and (c) demonstrate that the luminescence intensity for the detection of CC and tryptophol, when compared to the earlier results, exhibits a very Minor deviation. Collectively, these experiments highlight the remarkable stability and reproducibility demonstrated by the developed ECL sensor.

Regarding emission standards for phenol pollutants in surface water within the Beijing Municipality, according to the Water Pollution Control Standards for Discharge (DB11/301–2013), the permissible range is between 9.802 × 10-8 M and 1.816 × 10-6 M. Furthermore, literature indicates that tryptophol concentrations in Candida albicans samples vary from 1.520 × 10-5 M to 1.185 × 10-3 M [58]. Both analytes are encompassed within the linear range of detection established in our study, affirming the applicability of the developed ECL system for monitoring CC and tryptophol.

This study introduces a straightforward synthesis method using citric acid as the carbon source and L-cysteine as the doping source to successfully produce N, S-GQDs through a pyrolysis approach. The synthesized N, S-GQDs were thoroughly characterized using TEM, UV–Vis, and FL. Additionally, XRD was employed to measure diffraction patterns, and XPS provided in-depth analysis of surface functional groups, element content, and carbon forms. Subsequently, a paper-based ECL sensor was developed utilizing N, S-GQDs as the luminescent material and K2S2O8 as the co-reactant for the detection of CC and tryptophol. The sensor exhibited linear sensitivity to CC and tryptophol within the concentration range of 0.01–1000 μM, with corresponding detection limits of 0.0082 μM and 0.0066 μM, suitable for a wide range of monitoring requirements. The study underscores the unique sensitivity and selectivity of N, S-GQDs, providing robust support for the reliable detection of CC and tryptophol. This technological advancement facilitates the rapid and accurate identification of CC and tryptophol in samples, offering crucial insights for timely implementation of control measures. The significance of these research findings extends to various fields, including water pollution monitoring and Candida albicans diagnosis, highlighting the comprehensive contribution of this study.

Shaoze Zhi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Zikang Zhu: Methodology. Yipeng Li: Investigation. Zhengchun Yang: Validation, Supervision, Methodology. Jun Liu: Project administration. Junjun Shi: Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis. Peng Pan: Conceptualization. Yangyang Qi: Resources, Project administration. Jie He: Funding acquisition, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the support by Key Research and Development Program of Tianjin City(Grant No. 22YFYSHZ00280), National Engineering Research Center for Petroleum Refining Technology and Catalyst (RIPP, SINOPEC), Tianjin Natural Science Foundation (Grant No.18JCZDJC99800), National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51502203), Tianjin Distinguished Professor Foundation of Young Researcher, and Tianjin Development Program for Innovation and Entrepreneurship.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110408.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
